preFix.title: Rya pile inserts in tapestry weave
preFix.subtitle: Weaving cut-pile rya knots into tapestry-weave ground to add textured relief sections
preFix.excerpt: The rya knot ties a tuft of yarn around two warp threads between picks of tabby ground. In tapestry work, rows of rya knots build textured pile sections within a flat-woven design, adding sculptural relief and contrast of texture.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from tapestry weaving references and public-domain documentation of the rya (ghiordes / Turkish) knot technique.

content.text: Before the technique instructions, here is a plain-English description of the structure.
Tapestry weave
is a
tabby
(plain weave) structure in which the
weft
fully covers the
warp
. Different coloured wefts are used to build areas of colour and pattern. A
rya knot
is a separate operation performed between
pick
s of tabby ground rather than during a weaving pass. Each knot adds a tuft of yarn that stands up from the flat woven surface, creating
pile
sections of sculptural texture within an otherwise flat tapestry.
This technique is used on upright tapestry looms. The
sett

for tapestry is typically 6 to 10 EPI (2.5 to 4 ends/cm) using medium-to-thick linen or cotton warp. Rya pile yarn can be any thick, textured, or multi-ply yarn: rya wool, thick singles, or bundles of thinner yarns held together. The pile length is determined by the cut length of the rya strands before knotting.

items.name: Linen or cotton warp, 12/6 or similar
Rya pile yarn, thick or multi-ply
Blunt darning needle (for weaving in ends)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut rya strands to length

Decide the intended pile height: for a lush pile, 6 to 8 cm is a generous length; for a shorter, clipped pile, 3 to 4 cm. Cut all rya strands to twice the intended pile height plus 2 cm. A strand for a 6 cm pile should be cut to 14 cm. Wrap the yarn around a piece of card cut to the intended strand length and cut along one edge to produce even lengths in quantity.
Step 2: Weave tabby ground rows to the knot row
Weave two picks of
tabby

ground before beginning any row of rya knots. These ground picks separate the pile rows and lock each row of knots in place. Without the ground picks between pile rows, the knots can shift or loosen over time.
Step 3: Tie the rya knot

To tie a rya knot around two adjacent warp threads: fold one pre-cut strand in half over the two warp threads so the loop sits at the top and the two tails hang below. Working from the front of the warp, bring each tail under and up through the gap between the two warp threads (the left tail through the left gap and the right tail through the right gap) so that both tails emerge pointing downward from the front of the warp. Pull both tails firmly downward to tighten the knot. The knot should grip the two warp threads without sliding. Do not pull so hard that the warp threads are distorted.
Step 4: Continue across the pile row

Move to the next pair of warp threads and tie the next knot. Continue working from left to right (or right to left: consistency matters more than direction) across the intended pile section. Pile does not have to extend across the full warp width: rya knots can be tied in defined areas to create textured zones within a flat tapestry design.
Step 5: Lock each pile row with tabby

After completing a row of rya knots, weave two picks of tabby ground before adding the next row of pile knots. Use the tapestry comb to beat the tabby picks down over the bases of the knot tails, locking each knot row in place. The
shed
for the tabby picks is opened in the usual way with the shed stick or heddle rod.
Step 6: Trim and shape the pile

After completing all pile rows, trim the pile to the intended length using sharp scissors. The tails from the rya knots will have been cut to the same length in step 1, but some may be slightly uneven after knotting. Hold a ruler against the pile and trim across to the intended height. For shaped pile sections, draw the outline lightly on paper, hold the paper against the pile, and trim following the outline. The pile can be trimmed while the work is still on the loom.
Step 7: Weave in ends and finish

Weave in all weft ends using a blunt darning needle, running each end back along a row of tabby for at least 3 cm before trimming close. Rya knot tails do not need to be woven in: the tabby rows lock them. When the tapestry is cut from the loom, the warp ends can be knotted in pairs and trimmed, or woven back into the tapestry body with a blunt darning needle.

items.problem: Rya knots sliding up or down the warp
items.solution: The tabby ground picks are not packed tightly enough between the pile rows. Beat the ground picks firmly before tying the next row of knots. Two firm picks are required between each pile row.

items.problem: Pile tails are uneven in length after knotting
items.solution: The pre-cut strands were not cut consistently, or they shifted during knotting. Cut a consistent strand length using a card guide, and smooth the tails downward after tightening each knot before moving to the next pair.

items.problem: Warp threads distorting where knots are tied
items.solution: The knots are being pulled too tight. The knot should grip the warp pair firmly but not compress or squeeze it. Ease off the tightening force.

items.problem: Pile sections sitting below the flat tapestry surface
items.solution: The rya pile yarn is too fine relative to the flat weft yarn. Use a thicker pile yarn, or bundle two or three strands together as a single rya strand to achieve a pile height that clears the tabby surface.
